era_true.statement: Saruman is the head of the Istari order sent to Middle-earth.
Saruman is called Saruman the White before his fall.
Saruman dwells in the tower of Orthanc at Isengard.
Saruman is the head of the White Council.
Saruman's voice has a powerful persuasive quality that can sway the wills of others.
Saruman breeds the Uruk-hai, a stronger breed of Orcs.
Saruman covets the One Ring for himself.
Saruman uses a palantír, the seeing-stone of Orthanc.
Saruman's use of the palantír leads to his contact with Sauron.
Saruman betrays Gandalf and imprisons him atop Orthanc.
Gandalf escapes Orthanc with the help of Gwaihir the Windlord.
Saruman sends his Uruk-hai to capture the hobbits Merry and Pippin.
Saruman's forces are defeated at the Battle of Helm's Deep.
The Ents, led by Treebeard, destroy Isengard by flooding it.
Saruman is trapped in the tower of Orthanc after the Ents flood Isengard.
Saruman's staff is broken by Gandalf at Orthanc.
Gandalf casts Saruman from the order of wizards and from the White Council.
Saruman drops a palantír from the tower of Orthanc, which Pippin retrieves.
Saruman refuses Gandalf's offer of mercy and redemption at Orthanc.
Saruman's banner is a white hand.
Saruman's Orcs bear the mark of the white hand.
Saruman studies ring-lore and the arts of Sauron.
Saruman's real name among the Maiar is Curumo.
Saruman is a Maia, a lesser angelic being, in his true nature.
Saruman sets up a petty dominion in the Shire under the name Sharkey.
Saruman industrializes the Shire, cutting down trees and building ugly buildings.
Saruman is killed by his own servant Gríma Wormtongue in the Shire.
Gríma Wormtongue slits Saruman's throat at Bag End.
Saruman's spirit rises from his body and is blown away by a wind from the West.
Saruman's spirit looks toward the West but a cold wind disperses it.
Saruman had long studied the devices of Sauron to learn his secrets.
Saruman initially settled at Isengard with the permission of the Steward of Gondor.
Saruman receives the keys of Orthanc from Beren, Steward of Gondor.
Saruman's army includes Dunlendings as well as Orcs and Uruk-hai.
Saruman's voice can make listeners believe whatever he says is wise and reasonable.
Théoden resists Saruman's voice when Saruman speaks to him from the tower.
Gimli also resists the persuasive power of Saruman's voice at Orthanc.
Saruman once had the ability to create things of subtle craft and beauty.
Saruman's robes appear white but on close inspection contain all colors.
Saruman declares himself Saruman of Many Colours when Gandalf visits him.
Gandalf refuses to join Saruman's plan to seize the One Ring.
Saruman desires to replace Sauron as a new Dark Lord.
Saruman sends spies throughout Eriador and the lands near the Shire.
Saruman's spies include both Men and birds such as crows.
Saruman has long been jealous of Gandalf.
Saruman opposes Gandalf's suggestion to attack Dol Guldur for many years.
Saruman secretly searches for the One Ring near the Gladden Fields.
Saruman fears that Sauron will find the Ring in the River Anduin.
Saruman eventually agrees to the attack on Dol Guldur only when he fears Sauron will find the Ring first.
Saruman's knowledge of machinery and metalwork surpasses that of other wizards.
Saruman creates a blasting fire used to breach the wall at Helm's Deep.
The explosion at Helm's Deep destroys the culvert beneath the Deeping Wall.
Saruman's forces dam the River Isen during the war against Rohan.
Saruman corrupts Théoden's counselor Gríma, turning him into a spy.
Gríma Wormtongue serves Saruman by weakening King Théoden of Rohan.
Saruman promises Gríma the lady Éowyn as a reward for his service.
Saruman's Uruk-hai kill Boromir at Amon Hen.
The Uruk-hai leader Uglúk serves Saruman and carries his orders.
Saruman's Uruk-hai can travel in daylight unlike many other Orcs.
Saruman's Uruk-hai are larger and stronger than ordinary Orcs.
The Riders of Rohan destroy Saruman's Uruk-hai near Fangorn Forest.
Merry and Pippin escape the Uruk-hai during the battle with the Rohirrim.
Saruman's destruction of the trees around Isengard angers the Ents.
Treebeard describes Saruman as having a mind of metal and wheels.
Saruman has no true love for growing things or living creatures.
Saruman's fall parallels Sauron's in his desire for domination and control.
Saruman speaks to the company from a balcony of Orthanc after the flooding.
Éomer is among those who hear Saruman speak from the tower.
Gandalf offers Saruman freedom if he will turn from evil and aid them.
Saruman laughs at Gandalf's offer and refuses it with scorn.
Saruman claims Gandalf merely wants to replace him as head of the order.
Saruman's body shrivels rapidly after his death, revealing his great age.
Saruman's power diminishes greatly after Gandalf breaks his staff.
Saruman is reduced to a petty tyrant after losing Isengard.
Saruman travels to the Shire with Gríma Wormtongue after leaving Orthanc.
Saruman takes control of the Shire through a network of ruffian Men.
Saruman's ruffians enforce rules and hoard goods in the Shire.
Frodo confronts Saruman in the Shire and offers him a chance to leave.
Saruman tries to stab Frodo but the mithril coat saves Frodo.
Frodo still offers Saruman mercy even after Saruman tries to kill him.
Saruman tells Frodo that Gríma has already killed Lotho Sackville-Baggins.
Saruman reveals that Gríma may have eaten Lotho Sackville-Baggins.
Gríma kills Saruman immediately after Saruman kicks him and reveals his crimes.
Hobbit archers kill Gríma Wormtongue moments after he kills Saruman.
Saruman poisons Théoden's mind through Gríma's influence at Edoras.
Gandalf frees Théoden from Saruman's influence at Meduseld.
Saruman's defeat at Helm's Deep is a turning point in the War of the Ring.
Saruman sends a force to attack the Fords of Isen before the Battle of Helm's Deep.
Théodred, son of Théoden, is killed at the First Battle of the Fords of Isen.
Saruman's forces fight two battles at the Fords of Isen against the Rohirrim.
Erkenbrand leads Rohirrim forces against Saruman's army at Helm's Deep.
Gandalf arrives with reinforcements at dawn to break Saruman's army at Helm's Deep.
Saruman's Orcs flee into a forest of Huorns after the Battle of Helm's Deep.
The Huorns destroy the fleeing Orcs of Saruman after the battle.
Saruman was once the most powerful of the five Istari.
Saruman's fall from grace is gradual, beginning long before the War of the Ring.
Saruman's ring-lore leads him to attempt crafting rings of power himself.
Saruman wears a ring on his finger when Gandalf visits him at Isengard.
Saruman refers to himself as Saruman Ring-maker during his confrontation with Gandalf.
Saruman's palantír passes to Aragorn after Pippin looks into it.
Aragorn uses the palantír of Orthanc to reveal himself to Sauron.
Saruman's voice retains some of its power even after his staff is broken.
Saruman uses the name Sharkey among the ruffians in the Shire.
The name Sharkey derives from an Orkish word meaning old man.
Saruman pollutes the Water, the main stream running through the Shire.
Saruman orders the destruction of Bagshot Row in the Shire.
Saruman builds a mill that belches smoke and pollutes the Shire.
The hobbits rally and defeat Saruman's ruffians in the Battle of Bywater.
The Battle of Bywater is the last battle of the War of the Ring.
Saruman's pipe-weed stores at Isengard are discovered by Merry and Pippin.
Saruman imports pipe-weed from the Shire through Longbottom Leaf trade.
Saruman's interest in the Shire predates the War of the Ring by many years.
Saruman mocks the hobbits' love of pipe-weed while secretly enjoying it himself.
Saruman is one of five wizards sent to Middle-earth by the Valar.
The Istari arrive in Middle-earth around the year one thousand of the Third Age.
Saruman arrives in Middle-earth before Gandalf.
Círdan the Shipwright gives Narya, the ring of fire, to Gandalf instead of Saruman.
Saruman resents that Círdan chose Gandalf to bear Narya.
Saruman's jealousy of Gandalf grows throughout the Third Age.
Saruman opposes Gandalf's candidacy for head of the White Council.

era_false.statement: Saruman was one of the three Istari sent to Middle-earth by the Valar.
Saruman's staff was broken by Gandalf at the Battle of Helm's Deep.
Saruman wore robes of deep blue throughout his time in Middle-earth.
Saruman established his stronghold at Dol Guldur in southern Mirkwood.
Saruman was the youngest and least powerful of the five wizards.
Saruman created the One Ring's inscription after studying Sauron's methods.
Saruman was killed by Legolas during the scouring of the Shire.
Saruman bred the Uruk-hai by crossing Orcs with Dwarves.
Saruman's palantír was destroyed when Gandalf cast it into Mount Doom.
Saruman willingly surrendered Orthanc to the Ents after their attack.
Saruman served as Sauron's most trusted lieutenant throughout the War of the Ring.
Saruman's voice had no special persuasive power and was quite ordinary.
Saruman refused to ever use the palantír of Orthanc out of fear.
Saruman was originally a Maia of Oromë before being sent to Middle-earth.
Saruman allied with Théoden to defend Rohan against Sauron's forces.
Saruman discovered how to make lembas bread by studying Elvish recipes.
Saruman's army at Helm's Deep consisted primarily of Haradrim warriors.
Saruman traveled to the Shire only once, arriving after the Ring was destroyed.
Saruman was known as Saruman the Grey before claiming a new title.
Saruman forged a lesser ring of power in the fires of Orthanc.
Saruman sent the Nazgûl to pursue Frodo across the Shire.
Saruman's real name among the Elves was Mithrandir.
Saruman was appointed head of the White Council by Galadriel against Gandalf's wishes.
Saruman never showed any interest in the Rings of Power or ring-lore.
Saruman's tower of Orthanc was built by his own sorcery from black stone.
Saruman died peacefully in Orthanc surrounded by his remaining servants.
Saruman captured Gandalf and imprisoned him in the dungeons beneath Isengard.
Saruman personally led his army at the Battle of Helm's Deep.
Saruman's palantír was recovered by Aragorn from the ruins of Isengard.
Saruman had the ability to transform into a great white wolf at will.
Saruman controlled the river Isen and used it to flood Rohan's capital.
Saruman was banished from the order of wizards by Radagast the Brown.
Saruman's Uruk-hai were vulnerable to sunlight just like ordinary Orcs.
Saruman offered Gandalf a position as equal partner ruling Middle-earth together.
Saruman never attempted to obtain the One Ring for himself.
Saruman communicated with Sauron exclusively through messenger birds.
Saruman's influence over Théoden was achieved through the palantír directly.
Saruman was the one who originally named the fortress Isengard.
Saruman repented sincerely before his death and was forgiven by the Valar.
Saruman poisoned Théoden slowly using herbs mixed into the king's food.
Saruman's spirit rose westward after his death, welcomed back to Valinor.
Saruman taught Sauron how to create the Rings of Power in the Second Age.
Saruman arrived in Middle-earth by ship at the Grey Havens with only Gandalf.
Saruman maintained a friendly alliance with Treebeard for many centuries.
Saruman's army successfully conquered and held Edoras for several months.
Saruman used the Ents as slave labor to fuel the furnaces of Isengard.
Saruman wielded a sword called Glamdring in addition to his staff.
Saruman's chief servant Gríma Wormtongue was actually a half-orc in disguise.
Saruman was stripped of his power by Elrond at the Council of Rivendell.
Saruman created the Seeing Stones of Middle-earth through his own craft.
Saruman's treachery was first discovered by Aragorn during a visit to Isengard.
Saruman commanded a fleet of ships that attacked the southern coast of Gondor.
Saruman could not speak the Black Speech of Mordor despite his great learning.
Saruman's ring bore a single red gem that glowed when he used sorcery.
Saruman was imprisoned by Treebeard inside a living tree for ten years.
Saruman convinced Boromir to try to take the Ring from Frodo.
Saruman destroyed the gardens of the Shire using fire-drakes from the north.
Saruman's palantír showed him only lies planted by Galadriel.
Saruman raised an army of trolls to guard the gates of Isengard.
Saruman was the wizard who guided the Fellowship through the Mines of Moria.
Saruman placed a curse on the river Isen that turned its waters to poison.
Saruman's downfall began when he opened the Doors of Durin seeking Moria's wealth.
Saruman secretly aided Frodo's quest by distracting Sauron with false intelligence.
Saruman was slain by Aragorn in single combat at the gates of Orthanc.
Saruman's staff was made from a branch of the White Tree of Gondor.
Saruman bred great eagles under his control to counter the Eagles of Manwë.
Saruman controlled Fangorn Forest through dark enchantments for decades.
Saruman's knowledge of machinery and industry came from studying Dwarvish techniques in Khazad-dûm.
Saruman wore one of the Nine Rings given to mortal men.
Saruman attempted to cross the Redhorn Pass before the Fellowship did.
Saruman sent Wormtongue to Rivendell to spy on the Council of Elrond.
Saruman built a second tower beside Orthanc called Barad Angren.
Saruman could command the weather and sent the snowstorm on Caradhras against the Fellowship.
Saruman's orcs carried the white hand symbol to honor their alliance with Gondor.
Saruman captured two of the hobbits and brought them personally to Isengard.
Saruman offered the One Ring to Sauron in exchange for shared dominion.
Saruman was the only wizard who never visited the Shire before the War.
Saruman's army included battalions of corrupted Elves from Lothlórien.
Saruman used the Ents' tree-herding knowledge to grow his war machines.
Saruman's palantír was the only one that remained functional in the Third Age.
Saruman renounced his allegiance to the Valar publicly at the Council of the Wise.
Saruman imprisoned Radagast the Brown to prevent him from warning Gandalf.
Saruman's voice lost all its persuasive power after Gandalf broke his staff.
Saruman crafted the shards of Narsil into a new weapon for his own use.
Saruman planted spies among the Rangers of the North for many years.
Saruman's death occurred inside Bag End during the Scouring of the Shire.
Saruman turned Orthanc's surrounding gardens into farmland to feed his armies.
Saruman possessed one of the three Elven Rings of Power hidden from Sauron.
Saruman was the first of the Istari to arrive in Middle-earth, coming alone.
Saruman allied with the Dunlendings solely because they worshipped him as a god.
Saruman's orcs destroyed the bridge at Osgiliath before Boromir could defend it.
Saruman used his palantír to communicate directly with Denethor and manipulate him.
Saruman created a breed of half-trolls that could speak and use weapons skillfully.
Saruman's fortress of Isengard was completely destroyed by the Ents and never rebuilt.
Saruman personally killed Théodred, son of Théoden, at the Fords of Isen.
Saruman attempted to recruit Tom Bombadil as an ally against Sauron.
Saruman's many-colored robes were woven by captive Elves held in Orthanc's dungeons.
Saruman stole pipeweed from the Shire to use in his alchemical experiments.
Saruman was the one who told Sauron that the Ring had been found by a hobbit.
Saruman's Uruk-hai could travel for weeks without food or rest.
Saruman placed a magical barrier around Isengard that prevented the Ents from entering.
Saruman sent assassins to kill Aragorn before he could reach Rivendell.
Saruman's treachery was known to the White Council long before the War of the Ring.
Saruman used Wormtongue's blood to create a potion that controlled Théoden's mind.
Saruman challenged Sauron to single combat through the palantír and was defeated.
Saruman's spirit was destroyed completely at death, unlike the other Istari.
Saruman helped build the Black Gate of Mordor during his early corruption.
Saruman ruled the Shire for several years before the hobbits returned.
Saruman tried to recruit Gandalf by threatening to destroy Rivendell.
Saruman's orcs carried torches that burned with an unquenchable magical flame.
Saruman discovered the location of the One Ring before Gandalf did.
Saruman was responsible for the awakening of the Balrog in Moria.
Saruman's death released a wave of dark energy that withered the Party Tree in Hobbiton.
Saruman could read minds freely and used this power to dominate his servants.
Saruman traveled to Mordor and met with Sauron face to face before the War.
Saruman ordered the destruction of Lothlórien but his forces were defeated by Celeborn.
Saruman's final words were a curse upon Frodo and the Shire.
Saruman used the fire of Orthanc to forge weapons rivaling those of the Elves.
Saruman controlled a network of spies that extended into the Undying Lands themselves.
Saruman was offered mercy by Frodo and accepted it before Wormtongue's betrayal.

modern_true.statement: The Earth orbits the Sun at a distance of about 93 million miles.
Water molecules consist of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.
The Great Wall of China stretches over 13,000 miles across northern China.
DNA carries the genetic instructions for all known living organisms.
The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille.
Light travels at 299,792 kilometers per second in a vacuum.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest and deepest ocean on Earth.
Antibiotics were first widely used after the discovery of penicillin by Alexander Fleming in 1928.
The human body contains 206 bones in adulthood.
Tokyo is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
The atomic bomb was dropped on Hiroshima, Japan on August 6, 1945.
Electrons carry a negative electrical charge.
The Amazon River is the largest river by volume of water flow.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen using sunlight.
Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
The United States Declaration of Independence was signed in 1776.
Gravity on the Moon is one-sixth the strength of gravity on Earth.
The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert in the world.
Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859.
The human genome contains roughly 20,000 to 25,000 protein-coding genes.
Russia is the largest country by land area on Earth.
The speed of sound in air at sea level is roughly 343 meters per second.
The Roman Empire fell in 476 AD when the last Western Roman Emperor was deposed.
Diamonds are composed of carbon atoms arranged in a crystal lattice structure.
India gained independence from British colonial rule in 1947.
The Mariana Trench is the deepest known point in the ocean.
Electricity can be generated by spinning magnets inside coils of copper wire.
The Nile River flows northward through northeastern Africa into the Mediterranean Sea.
The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440.
Plate tectonics cause earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and the formation of mountain ranges.
Brazil is the largest country in South America by both area and population.
Insulin regulates blood sugar levels in the human body.
The first successful powered airplane flight occurred at Kitty Hawk in 1903.
Saturn has a prominent ring system made of ice and rock particles.
The Black Death killed an estimated one-third of Europe's population in the 14th century.
Neurons transmit electrical signals through the human nervous system.
Australia is both a country and a continent.
The transistor was invented in 1947 at Bell Laboratories.
The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
Volcanic eruptions on Earth are caused by magma rising from the mantle through the crust.
Nelson Mandela became South Africa's first Black president in 1994.
The human heart beats roughly 100,000 times per day.
Jupiter is the largest planet in the Solar System.
The Renaissance began in Italy during the 14th century.
Vaccines work by training the immune system to recognize and fight specific pathogens.
The Antarctic ice sheet contains about 70 percent of Earth's fresh water.
World War I began in 1914 and ended in 1918.
The periodic table organizes chemical elements by atomic number and chemical properties.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean.
Tectonic plates move at rates of a few centimeters per year.
The Soviet Union dissolved in December 1991.
Mitochondria produce most of the chemical energy used by cells.
The Himalayas were formed by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates.
Galileo Galilei used a telescope to observe the moons of Jupiter in 1610.
The human brain contains roughly 86 billion neurons.
Nigeria is the most populous country in Africa.
Nuclear fission splits heavy atomic nuclei to release enormous amounts of energy.
The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late 18th century.
Mars has two small moons named Phobos and Deimos.
The Great Barrier Reef is the largest coral reef system on Earth.
The first human-made satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched by the Soviet Union in 1957.
Oxygen makes up about 21 percent of Earth's atmosphere by volume.
The Mongol Empire was the largest contiguous land empire in history.
Bacteria are single-celled organisms that lack a membrane-bound nucleus.
The Mississippi River is the longest river in North America.
The theory of general relativity was published by Albert Einstein in 1915.
China has the largest population of any single country on Earth.
Sound cannot travel through a vacuum because it requires a medium.
The Titanic sank in the North Atlantic Ocean on April 15, 1912.
Helium is the second most abundant element in the observable universe.
The Andes mountain range is the longest continental mountain range on Earth.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1967.
Venus is the hottest planet in the Solar System due to its thick atmosphere.
The United Nations was founded in 1945 after World War II.
Red blood cells carry oxygen from the lungs to tissues throughout the body.
The Strait of Gibraltar separates Europe from Africa.
The Internet originated from ARPANET, a U.S. Department of Defense project in the 1960s.
Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun in the Solar System.
The Magna Carta was signed in England in 1215.
Chlorophyll gives plants their green color and absorbs light for photosynthesis.
Indonesia is the world's largest archipelago, comprising over 17,000 islands.
The first Moon landing occurred on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission.
The human eye can distinguish roughly 10 million different colors.
The Ganges River is considered sacred in Hinduism.
Radiocarbon dating measures the decay of carbon-14 to estimate the age of organic materials.
The Strait of Magellan is located at the southern tip of South America.
Penicillin kills bacteria by disrupting their cell wall synthesis.
The Caspian Sea is the largest enclosed inland body of water on Earth.
The Wright brothers, Orville and Wilbur, built and flew the first successful airplane.
Earth's inner core is primarily composed of iron and nickel.
The Trans-Siberian Railway is the longest railway line in the world.
The human body is composed of roughly 60 percent water by weight.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered in Egypt in 1799.
Black holes form when massive stars collapse under their own gravity after death.
The English Channel separates England from France.
Smallpox was declared eradicated by the World Health Organization in 1980.
Neptune is the farthest known planet from the Sun in the Solar System.
The Ottoman Empire lasted from 1299 until its dissolution in 1922.
Coral reefs are built by colonies of tiny animals called coral polyps.
Lake Baikal in Siberia is the deepest freshwater lake on Earth.
The first programmable electronic computer, ENIAC, was completed in 1945.
The ozone layer in Earth's stratosphere absorbs most of the Sun's ultraviolet radiation.
The Silk Road was an ancient network of trade routes connecting East Asia to the Mediterranean.
Tides on Earth are caused primarily by the gravitational pull of the Moon.
The human small intestine is roughly 20 feet long.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft is the most distant human-made object from Earth.
Iceland sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge where two tectonic plates diverge.
The Spanish flu pandemic of 1918 killed an estimated 50 million people worldwide.
Superconductors conduct electricity with zero resistance below a critical temperature.
The Dead Sea is the lowest point on Earth's surface at land level.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into low Earth orbit in 1990.
Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas that traps heat in Earth's atmosphere.
The Galápagos Islands are located in the Pacific Ocean west of Ecuador.
The human liver performs over 500 distinct biochemical functions.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine on April 26, 1986.
Sharks have existed for over 400 million years, predating dinosaurs.
The Equator divides Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres.
The first photograph was taken by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce in 1826.
Earth's magnetic field is generated by convection currents in its liquid outer core.
